title: LACTATIONAL MASTITIS and Continued BREASTFEEDING SUPPORT in a 29-Year-Old First-Time Mother 16 Days Postpartum Who Presents with a Unilateral, Wedge-Shaped, Red, Warm, Painful Area of the Right Breast Plus Fever 38.9°C, Chills, and Flu-Like Body Aches After Milk Stasis (a Missed Overnight Feeding + a Tight Bra) and a Cracked Nipple: Recognizing INFECTIOUS Mastitis vs ENGORGEMENT vs a PLUGGED DUCT vs a BREAST ABSCESS vs Inflammatory Breast Cancer, the CORNERSTONE of EFFECTIVE MILK REMOVAL (CONTINUE Breastfeeding — the Milk Is SAFE for the Baby — Nurse Frequently q2–3h, Warm Compress/Shower BEFORE Feeds to Aid Letdown and COLD Compress AFTER for Pain), NSAIDs + Acetaminophen + Rest + Hydration, an S. AUREUS-Directed Antibiotic (Dicloxacillin/Cephalexin × 10–14 Days — Complete the FULL Course; Clindamycin/TMP-SMX if MRSA or Penicillin-Allergic), CORRECTING the Underlying Cause (Latch/Positioning, Avoiding Tight Bras and Skipped Feeds), Recognizing the ABSCESS Complication (Fluctuant Mass + Persistent Fever Despite 48–72h of Antibiotics → Ultrasound + Drainage), and NON-JUDGMENTAL Emotional Support with a LACTATION-CONSULTANT Referral — Because Telling Her to STOP Breastfeeding or WEAN Worsens Stasis and Drives Abscess Formation
subtitle: LACTATIONAL MASTITIS is an inflammation, usually with infection, of the breast that classically appears in the 2nd–3rd week postpartum when MILK STASIS (a missed/skipped feeding, oversupply, a tight bra, abrupt weaning, or poor latch) combines with bacterial entry through a CRACKED NIPPLE — Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism. This case follows L. Okonkwo, a 29-year-old G1P1 who is 16 days postpartum and exclusively breastfeeding, presenting with one day of FEVER (38.9°C), CHILLS, and FLU-LIKE MYALGIAS/MALAISE plus a UNILATERAL, localized, WEDGE-SHAPED area of the right breast that is RED, WARM, FIRM, and TENDER — after her baby slept through an overnight feeding and she wore a too-tight bra, on a background of a cracked right nipple. The nurse must RECOGNIZE the pattern and DISTINGUISH it from look-alikes: ENGORGEMENT is bilateral, generalized, and early (no/low-grade fever); a PLUGGED DUCT is a localized lump WITHOUT systemic symptoms; MASTITIS is localized PLUS systemic fever/flu-like illness; a BREAST ABSCESS is a fluctuant, well-defined tender mass with persistent fever despite 48–72h of antibiotics (needs ultrasound + drainage); and INFLAMMATORY BREAST CANCER (rare, with peau d'orange and no response to antibiotics) is considered only if it does not resolve. The CORNERSTONE of treatment is EFFECTIVE MILK REMOVAL — the mother should CONTINUE BREASTFEEDING because the milk is SAFE for the baby; she nurses FREQUENTLY (every 2–3 hours), often STARTING on the affected side, applies a WARM compress or takes a warm shower BEFORE feeds to aid letdown, and uses a COLD compress AFTER feeds for pain/swelling; she pumps only if the baby cannot empty the breast. Comfort and anti-inflammatory measures include IBUPROFEN (compatible with breastfeeding) and acetaminophen, plus REST and HYDRATION. Because she is systemically ill, an S. AUREUS-directed ANTIBIOTIC is started — DICLOXACILLIN or CEPHALEXIN for 10–14 days (CLINDAMYCIN or TMP-SMX if MRSA is suspected or she is penicillin-allergic; avoid TMP-SMX with a jaundiced/ill/G6PD-deficient or <1-month-old infant) — and she is taught to COMPLETE THE FULL COURSE even once she feels better. The UNDERLYING CAUSE is corrected: a lactation consultant evaluates LATCH and POSITIONING, treats the nipple trauma, and counsels against tight bras, skipped feeds, and abrupt weaning. Critically, telling a mother to STOP breastfeeding or to WEAN is WRONG — it worsens stasis and promotes abscess formation — and she needs NON-JUDGMENTAL emotional support and reassurance that breastfeeding can and should continue. Step-6 evaluates whether each later finding reflects the mother IMPROVING, WORSENING (e.g., a fluctuant abscess or sepsis), or remaining UNCHANGED.

patient.name: L. Okonkwo
patient.admitDate: 16 days postpartum (G1P1, spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant), exclusively breastfeeding. Presents to the OB/postpartum clinic with 1 day of fever, chills, and body aches and a tender red area of the right breast. History: her baby began sleeping longer and she missed an overnight feeding, she has been wearing a tight underwire bra, and she has a cracked, sore right nipple from a shallow latch. Otherwise well; no chest pain, no leg swelling.
patient.room: OB / Postpartum Clinic, Exam Room 2; infant with her, feeding well

vitals.spo2: 99% on room air; T 38.9°C; reports shaking chills and flu-like body aches since last night; right breast upper-outer quadrant with a wedge-shaped RED, WARM, FIRM, TENDER area; cracked right nipple; left breast soft and non-tender; pain 6/10 in the right breast

99% RA; T 38.7°C; localized erythema/induration of the right upper-outer quadrant, NO fluctuant mass and NO well-defined pocket on palpation (no abscess yet); no axillary fluctuance; skin without peau d'orange; baby latches and transfers milk on the affected side after positioning coaching

vitals.time: Day 2 — phone follow-up after starting treatment
vitals.spo2: 99% RA; T 37.6°C; on dicloxacillin + ibuprofen with frequent breastfeeding/warm-then-cold compresses; redness smaller, pain improving, body aches resolving — early response to effective milk removal + antibiotics
99% RA; T 37.0°C; afebrile, redness nearly resolved, breast soft after feeds, nipple healing with corrected latch; continuing to breastfeed comfortably and completing the antibiotic course

98% RA; T 38.8°C; despite 48–72h of antibiotics she has a persistent fever and now a FLUCTUANT, well-defined, exquisitely tender mass in the right breast — concern for a BREAST ABSCESS requiring ultrasound + drainage
99% RA; T 36.8°C; mastitis resolved, breastfeeding well established and comfortable, latch corrected, no recurrence; completed the full antibiotic course

labs.n: PRESENTATION + WHY mastitis happens (milk stasis + a portal of entry)
labs.v: 29 y/o, 16 days postpartum, exclusively breastfeeding, with a unilateral WEDGE-SHAPED red/warm/firm/tender right breast PLUS fever 38.9°C, chills, and flu-like myalgias after a MISSED overnight feeding and a tight bra, on a background of a CRACKED nipple. KEY PRINCIPLE: lactational mastitis = MILK STASIS (skipped/missed feeds, oversupply, tight bra/underwire, abrupt weaning, poor latch, fatigue) + bacterial entry through a CRACKED/fissured nipple. Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) is the most common organism. It typically appears in the 2nd–3rd postpartum week. Recognize it as UNILATERAL + LOCALIZED + SYSTEMIC (fever/flu-like) — distinct from bilateral engorgement and from a plugged duct without systemic symptoms.
labs.r: Mastitis = MILK STASIS (missed feeds, oversupply, tight bra, abrupt weaning, poor latch) + bacteria entering via a CRACKED nipple; most common organism S. aureus; classic in postpartum week 2–3; pattern is UNILATERAL + LOCALIZED wedge of redness/warmth/tenderness PLUS systemic fever/chills/body aches.

labs.n: DISTINGUISHING engorgement vs plugged duct vs mastitis vs abscess vs inflammatory cancer
labs.v: ENGORGEMENT: BILATERAL, generalized firmness/swelling, early (days 2–5), NO or low-grade fever — managed with frequent feeding, comfort measures, and reverse-pressure softening. PLUGGED (BLOCKED) DUCT: a LOCALIZED tender lump, often with a white bleb on the nipple, NO systemic symptoms or only low-grade — relieved by frequent feeding, warmth, massage toward the nipple, and varied positions. MASTITIS: a LOCALIZED wedge of redness/warmth/tenderness PLUS SYSTEMIC fever ≥38.5°C, chills, and flu-like aches — the case here. BREAST ABSCESS: a FLUCTUANT, well-defined, exquisitely tender mass with PERSISTENT fever despite 48–72h of appropriate antibiotics — needs ULTRASOUND and DRAINAGE (needle aspiration or incision & drainage); breastfeeding can usually continue. INFLAMMATORY BREAST CANCER: rare, with peau d'orange, skin thickening/dimpling, and NO response to antibiotics — consider/refer if the 'mastitis' does not resolve.
labs.r: ENGORGEMENT = bilateral/generalized, early, little/no fever. PLUGGED DUCT = localized lump, NO systemic symptoms. MASTITIS = localized redness + SYSTEMIC fever/flu-like. ABSCESS = fluctuant mass + persistent fever despite 48–72h antibiotics → US + drainage. INFLAMMATORY CANCER = peau d'orange, no antibiotic response → refer if not resolving.

labs.n: CORNERSTONE — EFFECTIVE MILK REMOVAL: KEEP breastfeeding (the milk is SAFE)
labs.v: The single most important treatment is EFFECTIVE, FREQUENT MILK REMOVAL to relieve stasis. The mother should CONTINUE BREASTFEEDING — the milk is SAFE for the baby and stopping makes mastitis WORSE and promotes abscess formation. Nurse FREQUENTLY (every 2–3 hours and on demand), often STARTING on the affected breast (or alternating to ensure it drains), ensure the breast is well emptied, and pump/hand-express only if the infant cannot empty it. Apply a WARM compress or take a warm shower BEFORE feeding to aid letdown and flow, gently massage the firm area toward the nipple during the feed, vary positions to drain all ducts, and use a COLD compress AFTER feeding to reduce pain and swelling. AVOID tight bras/underwires and abrupt weaning. This is teaching that an RN or LVN can reinforce in stable postpartum care.
labs.r: EFFECTIVE MILK REMOVAL is the cornerstone: CONTINUE breastfeeding (milk is SAFE; stopping worsens it/causes abscess), nurse q2–3h often starting on the affected side, fully empty the breast (pump only if baby can't), WARM compress/shower BEFORE to aid letdown + gentle massage toward the nipple + varied positions, COLD compress AFTER for pain; avoid tight bras and abrupt weaning.

labs.n: ANTIBIOTICS, analgesia, supportive care, and CORRECTING the cause
labs.v: Because she is SYSTEMICALLY ill (fever, chills, myalgias) and has localized findings, start an S. AUREUS-directed oral antibiotic: DICLOXACILLIN or CEPHALEXIN for 10–14 days; if MRSA is suspected or she is penicillin-allergic, use CLINDAMYCIN or TMP-SMX (AVOID TMP-SMX if the infant is <1 month old, jaundiced/ill, or G6PD-deficient). Teach her to COMPLETE THE FULL COURSE even once she feels better. For pain/inflammation/fever use IBUPROFEN (an NSAID, compatible with breastfeeding and helpful for inflammation) and/or ACETAMINOPHEN. Support with REST, increased FLUIDS, and help at home. CORRECT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE with a LACTATION CONSULTANT: fix LATCH and POSITIONING, treat nipple trauma, and address oversupply, tight bras, and skipped feeds. Reassess in 24–48h; if no improvement or a fluctuant mass develops, evaluate for an ABSCESS (ultrasound + drainage).
labs.r: Systemic illness → S. aureus-directed antibiotic: DICLOXACILLIN or CEPHALEXIN ×10–14d (CLINDAMYCIN/TMP-SMX if MRSA or PCN-allergic; avoid TMP-SMX with a young/jaundiced/G6PD infant) — COMPLETE the full course. IBUPROFEN ± acetaminophen for pain/fever; REST + fluids. Fix the CAUSE via lactation consult (latch, positioning, nipple care). Reassess 24–48h; no improvement/fluctuant mass → abscess workup (US + drainage).

labs.n: EMOTIONAL SUPPORT, RED FLAGS, and PERTINENT NEGATIVES (what NOT to do)
labs.v: Provide NON-JUDGMENTAL emotional support — many mothers feel guilt or consider quitting; reassure her that mastitis is common, treatable, and that breastfeeding can and SHOULD continue. Teach RED FLAGS to report: worsening or spreading redness, a fluctuant lump, no improvement after 24–48h of antibiotics, high persistent fever, or the baby refusing to feed. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice — these are WRONG): do NOT tell her to STOP breastfeeding or WEAN (worsens stasis → abscess); do NOT 'pump and dump' or discard the milk as unsafe; do NOT advise tight binding of the breasts to 'dry up' the milk; do NOT apply only cold and stop emptying the breast; do NOT withhold antibiotics in a systemically ill mother or tell her to stop them once she feels better; and do NOT skip correcting the latch/positioning that caused the nipple trauma.
labs.r: Give NON-JUDGMENTAL support; reassure breastfeeding can/should continue. RED FLAGS: spreading redness, fluctuant lump, no improvement in 24–48h, persistent high fever, baby not feeding. NEGATIVES (WRONG): don't tell her to stop/wean, don't call the milk unsafe or pump-and-dump, don't tightly bind to dry up milk, don't stop emptying the breast, don't withhold/prematurely stop antibiotics, don't ignore the latch.

labs.v: 13.2 x10^9/L with mild neutrophilia (modest leukocytosis consistent with a localized bacterial infection; mastitis is a clinical diagnosis — labs are not required in a typical outpatient case)
38.9°C (102.0°F) — meets the systemic-illness threshold (≥38.5°C) that, with localized breast findings, defines mastitis rather than a simple plugged duct
Mildly elevated (supports an inflammatory/infectious process; not routinely needed but can be drawn if the diagnosis is unclear)
11.6 g/dL (mild postpartum anemia; pertinent to fatigue but not the cause of mastitis)

labs.n: Breast-milk / abscess culture (only if not responding or recurrent)
labs.v: Not obtained at this routine visit; reserved for severe, hospital-acquired, recurrent, or non-responding cases, or to identify MRSA and guide antibiotics — would be sent with any abscess drainage (pending if/when collected)

nursesNote: Clinic — 16 days postpartum, G1P1, exclusively breastfeeding. Presents with 1 day of fever 38.9°C, chills, and flu-like body aches plus a UNILATERAL wedge-shaped RED/WARM/FIRM/TENDER area of the RIGHT upper-outer breast; cracked right nipple; left breast soft. History of a MISSED overnight feeding and a tight underwire bra (milk stasis) with a shallow latch — classic LACTATIONAL MASTITIS, not bilateral engorgement and not a symptom-free plugged duct. On exam NO fluctuant mass and no peau d'orange (no abscess/inflammatory-cancer features today). Taught the cornerstone — EFFECTIVE MILK REMOVAL: CONTINUE breastfeeding because the milk is SAFE; nurse every 2–3 hours, often starting on the affected side and fully emptying the breast; WARM compress/shower BEFORE feeds to aid letdown with gentle massage toward the nipple and varied positions; COLD compress AFTER feeds for pain; avoid tight bras and abrupt weaning. Reinforced IBUPROFEN (compatible with breastfeeding) ± acetaminophen, REST, and increased fluids. Provider started dicloxacillin for 10–14 days (cephalexin alternative; clindamycin/TMP-SMX if MRSA or penicillin-allergic, avoiding TMP-SMX with a young/jaundiced/G6PD infant) — taught to COMPLETE the full course even when feeling better. Lactation consultant engaged to correct latch/positioning and treat the nipple trauma. Provided non-judgmental support and reassurance that breastfeeding should continue, and taught RED FLAGS to report: spreading redness, a fluctuant lump, no improvement in 24–48h, persistent high fever, or the baby refusing to feed. Did NOT advise stopping/weaning, did NOT call the milk unsafe or recommend pump-and-dump, did NOT advise tight binding, and did NOT stop emptying the breast. Will reassess in 24–48h; a fluctuant mass with persistent fever would prompt ultrasound + drainage for a breast abscess.
providerOrders: DIAGNOSIS: lactational mastitis of the right breast — clinical diagnosis; routine labs/imaging not required in an uncomplicated outpatient case

CONTINUE BREASTFEEDING: the milk is safe for the infant; nurse frequently (every 2–3 hours), often starting on the affected side, and ensure the breast is well emptied (pump/hand-express only if the infant cannot empty it)

COMFORT/MILK FLOW: warm compress or warm shower BEFORE feeds to aid letdown with gentle massage toward the nipple and varied feeding positions; COLD compress AFTER feeds for pain and swelling; avoid tight bras/underwires and abrupt weaning

ANTIBIOTIC: dicloxacillin or cephalexin PO for 10–14 days (S. aureus coverage); clindamycin or TMP-SMX if MRSA suspected or penicillin-allergic — avoid TMP-SMX if the infant is <1 month old, jaundiced/ill, or G6PD-deficient; complete the FULL course
ANALGESIA/ANTIPYRETIC: ibuprofen (NSAID, breastfeeding-compatible, anti-inflammatory) and/or acetaminophen for pain and fever
SUPPORTIVE CARE: rest, increased oral fluids, and help with home/infant care
LACTATION CONSULT: evaluate and correct latch and positioning, treat nipple trauma, and counsel on preventing milk stasis (avoid skipped feeds, tight bras, oversupply, abrupt weaning)

FOLLOW-UP/ESCALATION: reassess in 24–48 hours; if no improvement, worsening/spreading erythema, or a fluctuant mass with persistent fever after 48–72h of antibiotics, obtain a BREAST ULTRASOUND and arrange DRAINAGE (needle aspiration or incision & drainage) with culture

EDUCATION/RED FLAGS: report spreading redness, a fluctuant lump, no improvement after 24–48h, persistent high fever, or the baby refusing to feed; provide non-judgmental reassurance that breastfeeding can and should continue

opts.text: A unilateral, wedge-shaped, RED, WARM, FIRM, TENDER area of the right breast
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a localized, unilateral wedge of inflammation is the hallmark of mastitis (versus bilateral, generalized engorgement).

opts.text: Fever 38.9°C with chills and flu-like body aches (malaise/myalgias)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — systemic flu-like symptoms with fever ≥38.5°C distinguish mastitis from a symptom-free plugged duct.

opts.text: A cracked right nipple from a shallow latch
opts.rat: RELEVANT — nipple fissures are the portal of entry for S. aureus and a correctable cause.

opts.text: A missed overnight feeding and a tight underwire bra (milk stasis)
opts.rat: RELEVANT — milk stasis from skipped feeds and external compression is the key precipitant of mastitis.

opts.text: Onset at 16 days postpartum (the typical 2nd–3rd week window)
opts.rat: RELEVANT — the timing fits the classic presentation of lactational mastitis.

opts.text: Both breasts are equally firm and swollen with no fever
opts.rat: ⚠️ This describes ENGORGEMENT, not mastitis — her left breast is soft and she has a unilateral, febrile, localized process.

opts.text: A fluctuant, well-defined mass with skin that has a peau d'orange (orange-peel) appearance
opts.rat: ⚠️ Not present today — a fluctuant mass suggests an abscess and peau d'orange suggests inflammatory cancer; her exam shows neither.

opts.text: This is lactational mastitis — milk stasis plus bacterial entry (usually S. aureus) through a cracked nipple, with systemic symptoms
opts.rat: CORRECT — the localized inflammation plus fever/flu-like illness and the stasis/nipple history define infectious mastitis.

opts.text: The cornerstone of treatment is EFFECTIVE MILK REMOVAL — she should continue breastfeeding because the milk is safe and stopping worsens it
opts.rat: CORRECT — relieving stasis with continued, frequent, effective emptying is the most important intervention.

opts.text: Because she is systemically ill, an S. aureus-directed antibiotic (e.g., dicloxacillin or cephalexin) is indicated for 10–14 days
opts.rat: CORRECT — systemic symptoms warrant antibiotics covering S. aureus, completed as a full course.

opts.text: Correcting the underlying cause (latch/positioning, avoiding tight bras and skipped feeds) helps treat and prevent recurrence
opts.rat: CORRECT — addressing nipple trauma and stasis with lactation support is essential to resolution and prevention.

opts.text: If a fluctuant mass and persistent fever develop despite 48–72h of antibiotics, a breast abscess is likely and needs ultrasound and drainage
opts.rat: CORRECT — failure to improve with a fluctuant mass signals abscess formation requiring drainage.

opts.text: She should stop breastfeeding and wean to let the breast 'rest' and heal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Stopping or weaning increases milk stasis and promotes abscess formation — she must keep emptying the breast.

opts.text: The breast milk is unsafe for the infant during mastitis and should be pumped and discarded
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The milk is safe; continued breastfeeding is both safe and therapeutic.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these explanations from #1 (MOST likely/important now) to #5 (LEAST).

opts.text: Lactational (infectious) mastitis of the right breast
opts.rat: #1 — MOST likely: a unilateral localized wedge of inflammation PLUS fever and flu-like systemic symptoms is the defining picture.

opts.text: A plugged (blocked) milk duct
opts.rat: #2 — On the spectrum and often precedes mastitis, but a plugged duct lacks the systemic fever/flu-like illness she has.

opts.text: Breast engorgement
opts.rat: #3 — Less likely: engorgement is bilateral and generalized with little/no fever, whereas she has a unilateral febrile process.

opts.text: A breast abscess
opts.rat: #4 — A complication to watch for, but there is no fluctuant mass yet; it becomes likely only if she fails to improve.

opts.text: Inflammatory breast cancer
opts.rat: #5 — LEAST likely: rare, with peau d'orange and no antibiotic response — considered only if the 'mastitis' does not resolve.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Continue breastfeeding frequently (every 2–3 hours), often starting on the affected side, to fully empty the breast
opts.rat: Effective, frequent milk removal is the cornerstone of treatment and is safe for the infant.

opts.text: Apply a warm compress or take a warm shower before feeds, and a cold compress after feeds
opts.rat: Warmth before feeds aids letdown/flow; cold after feeds reduces pain and swelling.

opts.text: Take ibuprofen and/or acetaminophen for pain and fever
opts.rat: Both are compatible with breastfeeding; ibuprofen also helps the inflammation.

opts.text: Start an S. aureus-directed antibiotic (dicloxacillin or cephalexin) and teach her to complete the full course
opts.rat: Systemic illness warrants antibiotics; completing the course prevents relapse and resistance.

opts.text: Refer to a lactation consultant to correct latch/positioning and treat the nipple trauma, and rest/hydrate
opts.rat: Correcting the underlying cause and supporting recovery treat the current episode and prevent recurrence.

opts.text: Advise her to stop breastfeeding and wean to let the breast heal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stopping increases stasis and promotes abscess formation; she must keep emptying the breast.

opts.text: Tell her to pump and discard the milk because it is unsafe for the baby
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the milk is safe; direct breastfeeding is preferred and therapeutic.

opts.text: Apply a tight breast binder to suppress milk production
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — tight binding worsens stasis and pain and can precipitate an abscess.

opts.text: Reassure her the fever needs no treatment and she can stop antibiotics as soon as she feels better
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — she should complete the full antibiotic course; stopping early risks relapse and resistance.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the breasts and vital signs and confirm the pattern of mastitis (unilateral localized inflammation + systemic fever), ruling out a fluctuant abscess
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. A focused assessment confirms the diagnosis and screens for an abscess that would change management.

opts.text: Teach and support continued frequent breastfeeding/effective milk removal with warm-before/cold-after compresses
opts.rat: #2 — The cornerstone intervention; relieving stasis is the priority therapeutic action.

opts.text: Administer/teach the prescribed S. aureus-directed antibiotic and ibuprofen/acetaminophen, emphasizing the full course
opts.rat: #3 — Treats the infection and symptoms in a systemically ill mother.

opts.text: Refer to lactation support to correct latch/positioning and treat the nipple trauma, and advise rest and hydration
opts.rat: #4 — Addresses the underlying cause to resolve this episode and prevent recurrence.

opts.text: Teach red flags and arrange 24–48h follow-up, with ultrasound/drainage if an abscess develops
opts.rat: #5 — Ensures escalation if she fails to improve or an abscess forms.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each finding as the mother IMPROVING, WORSENING, or UNCHANGED.

opts.text: By day 2 her temperature is 37.6°C, the redness is smaller, and the body aches are resolving
opts.rat: Defervescence and shrinking erythema indicate the antibiotics plus effective milk removal are working.

opts.text: By day 4 she is afebrile, the breast is soft after feeds, and the nipple is healing with a corrected latch
opts.rat: Resolution of fever and inflammation with a healing nipple reflects successful treatment.

opts.text: She reports she is breastfeeding comfortably every 2–3 hours and emptying the breast well
opts.rat: Effective, frequent milk removal is relieving the stasis that drives mastitis.

opts.text: Despite 72 hours of antibiotics she has a persistent fever and a new fluctuant, well-defined, exquisitely tender mass
opts.rat: ⚠️ Signs of a BREAST ABSCESS — obtain an ultrasound and arrange drainage; antibiotics alone are insufficient.

opts.text: The redness is spreading rapidly with rigors, hypotension, and tachycardia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Signs of systemic deterioration/sepsis — escalate urgently for evaluation and possible IV antibiotics.

opts.text: She stopped breastfeeding on the affected side because it hurt, and the breast is now harder and more swollen
opts.rat: ⚠️ Stopping emptying increases stasis and worsens mastitis — resume frequent effective milk removal.

opts.text: A few hours after the first antibiotic dose her temperature is still 38.7°C
opts.rat: Fever does not break immediately; expect clinical improvement over 24–48 hours, so this is not yet a change.

opts.text: The localized tenderness is about the same on the evening of day 1
opts.rat: Early in treatment, stable findings are expected; continue therapy and reassess at 24–48 hours.

opts.text: At the 2-week follow-up the mastitis has resolved and breastfeeding is well established and comfortable
opts.rat: Full resolution with continued comfortable breastfeeding confirms effective treatment and prevention of recurrence.
